description: The Outdoor Capital of the UK — gateway to Ben Nevis, the Great Glen, and the Road to the Isles.

pois.name: Ben Nevis
pois.summary: Britain's highest mountain at 1345m — a deceptively flat-backed giant that has lured and humbled walkers for centuries. Around 125,000 people attempt the summit every year; the ruins of a Victorian weather observatory are still visible at the top.

trivia.title: The proposal to make it taller
trivia.body: A correspondent writing to the Glasgow Herald in the 1880s bemoaned the fact that Ben Nevis — Britain's highest mountain — was so lacking in distinction of shape. From most directions it appears as little more than a flat-backed lump. His solution: dynamite vast amounts of rock from neighbouring peaks and pile them on top to create a more graceful summit, raising it to 5,000 feet. 'The view would be superb,' he enthused. Happily, the Ben was saved from being turned into a slag heap by the indifference of the public.

trivia.title: The summit observatory
trivia.body: Between 1883 and 1904, a team of meteorologists lived year-round in a stone observatory at the summit of Ben Nevis — at 1345m, the most isolated weather station in the British Isles. They recorded readings every hour, in conditions that included winds of over 150mph and temperatures of -17°C. The data they collected transformed the understanding of Scottish mountain weather. The ruins of the observatory are still visible at the summit.

trivia.title: The annual race to the summit
trivia.body: Every September, the Ben Nevis Race sends runners from the town of Fort William to the 1345m summit and back — a round trip of 14 miles with 1350m of ascent. The record, set in 1984 by Kenny Stuart, stands at 1 hour 25 minutes and 34 seconds. The average runner takes around 3-4 hours. The race has been run since 1895, with a gap during the two World Wars, making it one of the oldest fell races in Britain. Competitors regularly disappear into cloud within the first few hundred metres of ascent.

pois.name: Glen Nevis
pois.summary: A dramatic glacial valley beneath Ben Nevis and one of Scotland's most-filmed landscapes — Braveheart, Rob Roy, and Harry Potter have all used it as a backdrop. The gorge at the head of the glen leads to Steall Falls, Scotland's second highest waterfall.

trivia.title: Braveheart & Harry Potter
trivia.body: Glen Nevis served as a key filming location for Mel Gibson's Braveheart (1995), with the lower glen standing in for the Scottish lowlands. The spectacular Steall Falls at the head of the glen — Scotland's second highest waterfall at 120m — appeared in the background during Quidditch match sequences in the Harry Potter films. The Wire Bridge crossing the gorge to Steall is one of the most photographed spots in Scotland.

trivia.title: Ice-carved landscape
trivia.body: Glen Nevis was carved by a glacier over 10,000 years ago. The narrow gorge at the head of the glen — the Nevis Gorge — is one of the most dramatic in Scotland, with walls rising almost vertically from the river. The glen holds one of the best surviving examples of ancient Caledonian pine forest, which once covered much of the Scottish Highlands before centuries of felling.

trivia.title: Rob Roy (1995)
trivia.body: Liam Neeson's Rob Roy was filmed extensively in Glen Nevis and the surrounding area, with the glen standing in for the 18th-century Scottish Highlands. The production used many of the same locations as Braveheart, which filmed in the area the same year. Glen Nevis has become the default backdrop for any period drama requiring untouched Highland landscape — its lack of modern infrastructure makes it uniquely useful to filmmakers.

pois.name: Neptune's Staircase
pois.summary: The UK's longest staircase lock — a flight of eight locks on the Caledonian Canal raising boats 20 metres in a single dramatic flight. Built by a shepherd's son who became Scotland's greatest engineer, and opened in 1822 to fight a war that was already over.

trivia.title: Thomas Telford's masterpiece
trivia.body: The Caledonian Canal was completed in 1822 to the designs of Thomas Telford, connecting the Atlantic to the North Sea through the Great Glen. Neptune's Staircase at Banavie is its most spectacular feature — eight consecutive locks raising boats by 20 metres, the whole structure stretching for 460 metres. It took 19 years to build, using an army of navvies, and cost £900,000 — a sum that horrified Parliament.

trivia.title: Built to avoid Napoleon
trivia.body: The canal was built primarily for military reasons — to allow Royal Navy ships to move between the Atlantic and North Sea without sailing around the north of Scotland, which was exposed to French privateers during the Napoleonic Wars. By the time it opened in 1822, Napoleon had been defeated and the strategic threat had passed. The canal was largely used for fishing boats instead.

pois.name: West Highland Museum
pois.summary: A small but fascinating museum in the heart of Fort William, packed with Highland memorabilia — including a secret portrait of Bonnie Prince Charlie that can only be seen in a cylindrical mirror. One of the most ingenious pieces of hidden art ever made.

trivia.title: The secret portrait
trivia.body: After the Jacobite defeat at Culloden, all things Highland were banned — including portraits of the exiled Bonnie Prince Charlie. The museum holds a remarkable workaround: a painting that looks like nothing more than a meaningless smear of colour on canvas — until viewed in a cylindrical mirror, which reflects a credible likeness of the Prince. It is one of the most ingenious pieces of hidden art ever made.

trivia.title: Fort William's dark history
trivia.body: The original fort was built in 1654 by General Monck during Cromwell's occupation of Scotland, and rebuilt in stone after the Glorious Revolution. It was named after the future King William III and served as a garrison to suppress the Jacobite Highlanders. The town that grew up around it was known as Gordonsburgh before taking the fort's name. The fort itself was demolished in 1864 to make way for the railway.

trivia.title: The room where the order was signed
trivia.body: Among the West Highland Museum's most remarkable exhibits is the original wooden paneling from the room in Inveraray Castle where the order for the Massacre of Glencoe was signed in 1692. The order authorised soldiers to 'put all to the sword under seventy' — meaning every MacDonald man under the age of 70 was to be killed. It was signed by the Master of Stair, John Dalrymple, who had already expressed his desire to 'extirpate that nest of thieves'. The paneling was removed and brought here as a physical record of one of the most infamous acts of betrayal in Scottish history.

pois.name: Glenfinnan Viaduct
pois.summary: The 21-arch curved viaduct made famous as the Hogwarts Express crossing — one of the most photographed railway structures in the world.

trivia.title: The Hogwarts Express
trivia.body: The Glenfinnan Viaduct first appeared in Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets (2002) and became a recurring landmark throughout the series — most memorably in Prisoner of Azkaban when Dementors board the train. The viaduct is crossed by the Jacobite steam train, which supplied its locomotive and rolling stock for the films. The viewpoint for the classic shot is a 45-minute walk from Glenfinnan station.

trivia.title: Built without steel
trivia.body: The viaduct was built in 1901 using mass concrete — an unusually advanced technique for the era. Engineer Robert McAlpine, known as 'Concrete Bob', pioneered the method partly because of a local shortage of skilled stonemasons. The 21 arches span 380 metres and stand up to 30 metres above the valley floor. More than a century later, the structure is in excellent condition.

pois.name: Glenfinnan Monument
pois.summary: A tall column topped by a kilted Highlander, marking the spot where Bonnie Prince Charlie raised his standard in 1745 — with Loch Shiel stretching hauntingly behind.

trivia.title: The standard is raised
trivia.body: On 19 August 1745, Charles Edward Stuart raised his standard at Glenfinnan before a gathering of Highland clans, marking the beginning of the Jacobite Rising that would sweep through Scotland before ending in catastrophe at Culloden 14 months later. At first only a handful of men had gathered — the Prince's supporters held their breath. Then the MacDonalds of Clanranald arrived, around 150 strong, followed shortly by the Camerons of Lochiel with 800 men. The rising had begun.

trivia.title: The inn where the Prince may have slept
trivia.body: The Prince's House Hotel in Glenfinnan dates to 1658 and was the only sizeable building in the area when Bonnie Prince Charlie was here in 1745. There is no documentary proof that he actually stayed the night — but as the hotel now puts it, 'it was the only sizeable house in Glenfinnan at the time, so…' The building is still an inn today.

pois.name: The Jacobite Steam Train
pois.summary: The 'Hogwarts Express' — a steam train running from Fort William to Mallaig across the Glenfinnan Viaduct, one of the greatest railway journeys in the world.

trivia.title: The real Hogwarts Express
trivia.body: West Coast Railways provided the route, carriages, and steam locomotives for every Harry Potter film featuring the Hogwarts Express. The specific locomotive used in the films — GWR 4900 Class 5972 Olton Hall, built in 1937 — now sits permanently on display at Warner Bros Studio Tour London. But the carriages used in filming are still in service on the Jacobite today, and the route crossing Glenfinnan Viaduct is identical. The morning First Class carriage even has period-style compartments that match the film interiors.

trivia.title: The Road to the Isles
trivia.body: The Fort William to Mallaig line — known as the Road to the Isles — was completed in 1901 and was one of the last great Victorian railway projects. It was built to open up the remote western Highlands and provide a rail connection to the Hebridean ferry port at Mallaig. The line crosses 11 viaducts, passes through 21 tunnels, and runs along the shores of three lochs. It is consistently voted one of the greatest rail journeys in the world.
